coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from biomass via simple one-pot method and exploration of their application
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Qiming
Duan, Jialong
Yang, Wen
Li, Xueming
Mo, Jinghui
Yang, Peizhi
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Pursuit of low-cost and large-scale method to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a persistent objective in recent years. In this work, we have successfully synthesized a series of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) under different hydrothermal temperature employing Eichhornia crassipes (ECs) as precursors. Considering the pollution ability to water and low-cost, this study may direct the novel path to convert waste material to useful quantum dots. After measurements such as TEM, XRD, Raman, XPS, PL as well as the UV–vis absorbance ability, outstanding optical properties have been discovered. In this fashion, solar cells are tentative to be fabricated, yielding the maximized solar-to-electrical conversion efficiency of 0.17% with a good fill factor of 67%. Meanwhile, the above-mentioned quantum dots also show the up-conversion ability, suggesting the potential application in infrared detection or broadening light-absorbing devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Eichhornia crassipes
Light absorber
One-pot method

Yunnan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2017FA024). Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2015EM024) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (201564002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Conversion processes from ECs to N-CQDs by a hydrothermal method. (b) Images of N-CQDs solutions synthesized at different temperatures with and without UV light illumination.
PL emission spectra of optimized N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths(a) 110 °C, (b) 140 °C, (c)180 °C, (d) 220 °C.
(a) Typical photo J–V characteristics curves of solar cells, (b) The energy band structures of N-CQD sensitized solar cell.
The typical TEM images of optimized N-CQDs at (a) low and (b) high resolutions. (c) The size distribution of optimized N-CQDs.
(a) XRD and (b) Raman spectrum of N-CQDs at a heating temperature of 140 °C.
XPS survey scan of N-CQDs on Si substrate. XPS full scan spectrum (a) and XPS high resolution survey scan of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s region. The heating temperature for N-CQDs is 140 °C.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectra of optimized N-CQDs with various dilution ratio. (b) The PL emission spectra of optimized N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs. (d) Up-convension flouresence spectra of N-CQDs. The heating temperature for N-CQDs is 140 °C.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from biomass via simple one-pot method and exploration of their application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technique & Preparation for Renewable Energy Materials, Ministry of Education, Yunnan Normal University, Kunming 650092, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Technique & Preparation for Renewable Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pursuit of low-cost and large-scale method to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a persistent objective in recent years. In this work, we have successfully synthesized a series of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) under different hydrothermal temperature employing Eichhornia crassipes (ECs) as precursors. Considering the pollution ability to water and low-cost, this study may direct the novel path to convert waste material to useful quantum dots. After measurements such as TEM, XRD, Raman, XPS, PL as well as the UV–vis absorbance ability, outstanding optical properties have been discovered. In this fashion, solar cells are tentative to be fabricated, yielding the maximized solar-to-electrical conversion efficiency of 0.17% with a good fill factor of 67%. Meanwhile, the above-mentioned quantum dots also show the up-conversion ability, suggesting the potential application in infrared detection or broadening light-absorbing devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In 2006, scientists at Claremonton University discovered and named a new type of carbon nanomaterials during the single-walled carbon nanotubes purification process: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [1]. As a new member of the carbon family, the emerging of CQDs has aroused great attentions in recent decades. Comparing to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, the preparation method of CQDs is simple and environmental-friendly, and the source of precursor materials is distributed widely and low-cost. In addition to maintaining the optical property of the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs also have excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, large two-photo absorption cross section and good photoinduced electron transfer ability [2,3]. As a result, they have been widely applied in many fields such as bioimaging, sensors, photoelectrocatalysis, fluorescence sensing, and solar cells [4–6].

Arising from the photo-excitation characteristics of CQDs, they are expected to be applied in sensitized solar cells as light absorbers. Comparing to traditional light-harvesting absorbers such as N719, N3, PbS or CdSe ect in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), the CQDs are advantageous owing to their health and environmental-friendless. The concept of using carbon nanoparticles as sensitizers for mesoscopic TiO2 solar cells was first reported by Yan et al. in 2010, achieving a power conversion efficiency of 0.056% [7]. Following, Mirtchev et al. reported a nanocrystalline TiO2-based solar cell sensitized with CQDs prepared by means of a carbonization synthesis, realizing an efficiency of 0.13% [8]. Since then, this work has been further researched for practical application.

However, reducing the production cost is still a challenging to prepare large-scale carbon quantum dots with low-cost. Therefore, during this development progress, enormous efforts have been put into developing green methodologies for preparing CQDs using natural substances widely distributed in nature [9,10], such as carbohydrates including glucose, sucrose, fructose [11] and biomass including orange juice, strawberry powders, watermelon peel and so on [12]. With the aim to accelerate the practical application of CQDs, how to utilize the useless material to prepare the precious CQDs is crucial. In the current work, we herein have prepared high-fluorescent water-soluble carbon quantum dots via one-pot method arising from the natural Eichhornia crassipes (ECs), which is a kind of discarded material for its pollution to pure water. There is a fact that the primary components of ECs include starch, cellulose and crude protein, which can be hydrolyzed into glucose and amino acid via hydrothermal treatment, therefore, playing a role of an excellent precursor for the preparation of CQDs. It should be noted that, according the elemental analysis, the as-prepared carbon quantum dots can be effectively doped by nitrogen atoms owing to no any further purification process for ECs before hydrothermal treatment, labeled as the N-CQDs, showing outstanding optical properties with unique up-conversion ability without any decoration. To further understand the application of this N-CQDs, we have experimentally fabricated the N-CQDs sensitized solar cells, yielding the lower power conversion efficiency from above-mentioned device in comparison to the state-of-art devices based on silicon, polymer, dye, CdS, CdSe, or perovskite. Till now, we can conclude that the strategy is proved an effective path to synthesize CQDs from biomass with expanded the precursors of CQDs.

N-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ECs as raw materials. In a representative synthesis, 0.3 g of ECs powder was added into 70 mL of deionized water. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at different temperatures (110, 140, 180 and 220 oC) for 6 h. The product was collected by filtration and centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large carbon product and finally freeze-dried under vacuum for 50 h. The obtained N-CQDs powders were dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 50 mg mL-1 for further characterization and usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-CQDs sensitized solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 photoanodes was prepared according to previous report [11]. The as prepared TiO2 electrode were immersed in 50 mg mL-1 concentrated N-CQDs aqueous solutions for 24 h to obtain N-CQDs sensitized TiO2 photo-anodes. The counter electrode was prepared by pyrolysising the 15mM chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6.6H2O) solution on the FTO glass substrate. The electrolyte solution composed of 0.1 M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M Tetrabutylammonium iodide, 0.1 M LiI, 0.1 M NaI, 0.1 M KI, 0.05 M I2 and 0.05 M 4-tertbutyl-pyridine in acetonitrile. Each solar cell device was fabricated by sandwiching a I-/I3 - redox electrolyte between a N-CQD sensitized TiO2 photoanode and a platinum counter electrode, which were sealed with a Surlyn film (25 μm in thickness).

The photocurrent-voltage (J-V) curves of N-CQDs sensitized solar cells with an active area of 0.25cm2 were recoded on a CHI660E electrochemical work station under irradation of simulated solar light from an intensity controlled to 100mWcm−2 xenon arc lamp. calibrated by a standard silicon solar cell. Each J-V curve was repeated measured at least 15 times using different samples to eliminate experimental errors.

The morphology of the N-CQDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G-20). The crystalline structure of N-CQDs was characterized by a X-ray diffraction (SHIMADZU XRD-6000, corporation, Japan) with Cu radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). Raman spectrum was obtained by a confocal Raman spectroscope (Perkin Elmer, UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using a UV600PC spectrometer (Scientific, China). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a JASCO/FT-IR-460 spectrometer ranged from 4000 to 400 cm-1. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-CQDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Japan) with the various excitation wavelength. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded on Thermo Scientific VGESCAlab MKII XPS system using Al Kα source. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) methods were used to measured the energy levels of N-CQDs via using a standard three-electrode system. The CV curves were scanned from -1.2 to 1.2 V at a scanning rate of 50 mV s-1. The LUMO and HOMO levels were obtained arising from the following to equations: E LUMO = -e(E red + 4.4) V, E HOMO = -e(E ox + 4.4) V.

Using ECs as raw materials, N-CQDs can be facilely synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process under various temperature ranged from 110 to 220 oC, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. A potential mechanism behind this conversion is that the primary components for ECs are starch, cellulose and crude protein, which will be hydrolyzed into glucose and amino acid during the hydrothermal reaction process for starch and cellulose as well as crude protein, respectively [13]. Subsequently, the resultants can be converted into N-CQDs through complicated dehydration condensation reactions [14]. Intriguingly, the color of resultant N-CQD water solution becomes deeper along with the elevation of reaction temperature (Fig. 1b), suggesting an increased N-CQD concentration. Meanwhile, the cyan photoluminescence can be observed obviously when suffering from UV illumination with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. There is obvious change in emission photoluminescence intensities under various heating temperature ranging from 110 to 220 oC.

In order to validate this phenomenon, these N-CQDs are characterized by the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 , the intensity of emission spectrum of N-CQDs at a heating of 140 °C are relatively lower, this result is consistent with the above phenomenon. In addition, with the increase of heating temperature, the maximum emission peak has a red shift, which is mainly attributed to the growth of quantum dots size along with the elevated temperature from 110 C to 220 oC. [10] UV–vis spectroscopy is a strong technique to determine the CQD growth process, as shown in Fig. S1. There are two characteristic absorption peaks for the N-CQD aqueous solutions at heating temperature 110 °C and 140 °C. When the heating temperature climb to 180 °C, the two peaks are transform into a broad absorption peak. This phenomenon demonstrated that the absorption characteristics are dependent on the crystalline of CQDs. [11]. The main peak of all N-CQDs is detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. S2, there is a broad characteristic peak about at 2θ = 30°, and the intensity of bond is increased gradually with growing temperature. The results showed that the size and crystalline of N-CQDs are increased with growing temperature.

Considering the excellent photo-excitation properties of N-CQDs, it is expected to be applied in optoelectronic devices. Therefore, we have experimentally fabricated the N-CQD sensitized solar cells as shown in Fig. S3, including a FTO supported N-CQD sensitized TiO2 photoanode, a I-/I3 - redox electrolyte, and a pyrolytic platinum counter electrode. Obviously, from Fig. 3a and Table S1, the device sensitized with N-CQDs prepared under 140 oC yields the champion solar-to-electrical conversion efficiency of 0.17% (exhibited a J sc of 0. 48 mA cm-2, a V oc of 525 mV and a FF of 67%), which is much higher than that of 0.07% (110 oC), 0.11% (180 oC) and 0.14% (220 oC). Meanwhile, the photovoltaic parameters obtained from previous studies and current work have been listed in Table S2 for comparison. To highlight the reasonable mechanism behind this phenomenon, the calculated energy band structures of various N-CQDs via CV method are illustrated in Fig. 3b. demonstrating the matchable energy level alignment for N-CQDs prepared under 140 oC as well as the accelerated electron transfer kinetics (Fig. S4 and Table S3). This conclusion can also be explained according to the incompleted honeycomb graphene structure and abundant oxygenic groups at a lower heating temperature (110 oC) and the weakened quantum effects as well as reduced electron excitation under illumination at elevated heating temperature (180 and 220 oC) [15].

Because of the excellent photoelectric properties of N-CQDs prepared under 140 °C, therefore the N-CQDs prepared under 140 oC have been chosen as a representative to study the detailed properties. Firstly, it has been subjected to TEM characterization, as shown in Fig. 4 a and b, showing well dispersibility and circular shape with the relatively uniform size of 1–2.5 nm (Fig. 4c), suggesting a feasible strategy to synthesize the N-CQD via hydrothermal method. The distances between the lattice fringes of N-CQDs are 0.252 nm from line-profile analysis (Fig. S5), which is similar to small pieces of graphitic layer stacking [15].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of as-prepared N-CQDs at a heating temperature of 140 °C is displayed in Fig. 5 a with a broad characteristic peak at 2θ = 22.8°, revealing an amorphous carbon phase, which is similar to the graphite lattice spacing, ascribing to highly disordered carbon atoms [16]. Additionally, as shown in the Raman spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 5b), there are two characteristic vibrations at 1373 and 1545 cm−1, which can be connected with a defect band (D band) and a graphite band (G band) [17]. The D band is associated with vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bands, while the G band is attributed to the vibration of sp2-banded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. The relative intensity ratio of two peaks about 0.889, reflecting the graphitization degree [18], which is agreement with the XRD results.

To cross-check the surface composition and element of the as-prepared N-CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are characterized as shown in Fig. 6 , revealing the existence of carbon (C1s, 284.9 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.9 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 530.9 eV). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6b) shows four peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 287.3 and 287.9 eV, which are assigned to bonds of CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. Fig. 6c illustrates three peaks of nitrogen at 398.5, 399.6 and 400.3 eV, which are attributed to the NH, CNC and CN(C)3 bands, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6d, oxygen 1s peak exhibits two peaks at 531.1 and 532.4 eV, which are attributed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. It can be concluded that N-CQDs have a variety of polar groups including hydroxyl, alkyl and carboxyl [17].

Fig. 7 a stacks the UV–vis absorption spectra of corresponding N-CQDs at various diluted aqueous solutions. The peak absorption intensities are proportional to the solution concentration, whereas the peak centers are nearly unchanged. It is obvious that there are two characteristic absorption peaks located at 280 nm and 325 nm as shown in UV–vis absorption spectra, which are typically attributed to the π → π* transition of CC segment [19] and the n → π* transition of CO bond [20], respectively. Fig. 7b shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of corresponding N-CQD solutions under various excitation wavelengths (λ ex) range from 320 to 480 nm. There is a fact that the PL intensity and maximal emission wavelengths (λ em) are highly dependent on λ ex, arising from the surface state of functional oxygenic groups (such as OH, CO, CO, CO-C, etc), which lead to various surface state energy levels between π and π* states of CC segments [10,21]. From corresponding PL excitation and emission spectrum as shown in Fig. S6, we can find that the maximal emission peak is located at 460 nm when subjecting an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The decoration of N-CQDs by oxygen-containing groups is determined by FTIR as show in Fig. 7c and the absorption bands of CN stretching vibrations at 1245 cm-1 indicates the successful doping of N atoms in the CQDs [9]. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks centered at 1663 and 3280 cm-1 are corresponding to the OH vibration and the COOH vibration, suggesting the formation of such oxygenic groups on the surface, which may be attributed to the effective interfacial charge transfer between TiO2 and quantum dots when applied in solar cells [22,23]. Besides, there are several other characteristic peaks centered at 2928, 1563, 1407, 1063 and 647 cm-1 are ascribed to CH stretching, CC stretching, COC stretching, CO stretching and CH2 stretching. Intriguingly, as shown in Fig. 7d, the as-prepared N-CQDs show the up-conversion ability when excited by longer wavelength ranged from 700 to 950 nm, which may be caused by the two or multi-photon active process [24]. Compared with previously reported carbon quantum dots [11,22], the outstanding optical properties of as-prepared N-CQDs is presented without any surface modification or additional chemical reagents, showing the promising application as the light absorber in photovoltaics.

In summary, we have employed a green and low-cost aquatic plants ECs to prepare cyan-fluorescence N-CQDs with uniform size about 1–2.5 nm via hydrothermal method. Enormous oxygenous functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl, endows the as-prepared N-CQDs water-soluble ability, facilitating the assemble process for many optoelectronic devices, such as the solar cells, which can yield the solar-to-electrical conversion efficiency of 0.17%. According to detailed characterizations, the N-CQDs show the up-conversion ability, suggesting the potential application in detecting or absorbing the near-infrared light.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (51362031, 21503202 and 61604143). Yunnan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2017FA024). Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2015EM024) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (201564002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.11.040

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
